questions.question:
Which of the following is TRUE about the interior gateway routing protocols — Routing Information Protocol (RIP) and Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)?

A. RIP uses distance vector routing and OSPF uses link state routing
B. OSPF uses distance vector routing and RIP uses link state routing
C. Both RIP and OSPF use link state routing
D. Both RIP and OSPF use distance vector routing
questions.answer: A. RIP uses distance vector routing and OSPF uses link state routing
questions.to_find: Which statement correctly describes the routing algorithms used by RIP and OSPF

understand.plain: RIP (Routing Information Protocol) and OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) are both interior gateway protocols (IGPs) used within an autonomous system. RIP is a classic distance-vector protocol where each router shares its routing table with neighbours. OSPF is a link-state protocol where each router floods information about its directly connected links to all routers, allowing every router to build a complete topology map.

keywords.explain: Each router advertises its routing table (distance to all destinations) to its immediate neighbours; routers update their own tables based on received information (Bellman-Ford algorithm).
keywords.example: RIP: router A tells neighbour B 'I can reach network X in 3 hops', B updates its table accordingly.

keywords.explain: Each router floods information about only its directly connected links (cost, neighbour identity) to every router in the domain; each router independently runs Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm on the resulting complete map.
keywords.example: OSPF: router A sends LSAs describing its links to all routers; every router builds a full graph and computes shortest paths with Dijkstra.

keywords.explain: A routing protocol used within a single autonomous system (organisation's internal network), as opposed to exterior protocols like BGP used between autonomous systems.
keywords.example: RIP and OSPF are IGPs; BGP is an EGP.

understand.visual_alt: RIP uses distance-vector (Bellman-Ford, periodic table broadcasts) while OSPF uses link-state (Dijkstra, LSA flooding). Both are interior gateway protocols but differ in algorithm and scalability.

given.aim: Identify the correct pairing of routing algorithm to routing protocol (RIP and OSPF)

terms.meaning: Routing Information Protocol — an IGP that uses distance-vector algorithm with hop count as metric (max 15 hops)
terms.example: Sends its full routing table to neighbours every 30 seconds using UDP port 520
terms.connects: Because RIP uses distance-vector, each router only knows costs advertised by neighbours, not the full topology

terms.meaning: Open Shortest Path First — an IGP that uses link-state algorithm (Dijkstra's SPF); routers flood Link State Advertisements (LSAs)
terms.example: OSPF runs directly over IP (protocol number 89) and supports areas for scalability
terms.connects: Because OSPF uses link-state, every router has a complete topology map and can compute optimal paths independently

terms.meaning: Distance-vector protocols (RIP) are based on Bellman-Ford; link-state protocols (OSPF) run Dijkstra's algorithm locally
terms.example: Bellman-Ford: iterative relaxation over neighbours; Dijkstra: greedy shortest-path on full graph
terms.connects: Confirms RIP -> distance vector, OSPF -> link state

given.plan: Recall the fundamental design of RIP (distance-vector, periodic updates via UDP) and OSPF (link-state, LSA flooding, Dijkstra). Match each protocol to its routing algorithm and select the correct option.

steps.title: Analyse RIP
steps.apply: RIP is one of the oldest IGPs. It uses the Bellman-Ford distance-vector algorithm: each router periodically broadcasts its entire routing table to neighbours. Metric is hop count (max 15). RIP messages are sent over UDP port 520. => RIP uses distance-vector routing.
steps.note: Distance-vector means routers only see the 'distance' and 'direction' to a destination, not the full path; this can cause count-to-infinity issues.

steps.title: Analyse OSPF
steps.apply: OSPF is a more advanced IGP. Each OSPF router floods Link State Advertisements (LSAs) describing its directly connected links to every router in the area. Every router builds an identical Link State Database (LSDB) and runs Dijkstra's SPF algorithm to compute shortest paths. OSPF runs directly over IP (not TCP or UDP). => OSPF uses link-state routing.
steps.note: Link-state protocols converge faster than distance-vector protocols and support VLSM/CIDR natively.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Option A states: 'RIP uses distance vector routing and OSPF uses link state routing.' This matches our analysis exactly. Options B, C, D are all incorrect.
steps.note: This is a standard exam fact: RIP = distance vector; OSPF = link state.

solution.result: A. RIP uses distance vector routing and OSPF uses link state routing

questions.formula_note: Distance-vector routing (Bellman-Ford, used by RIP) vs link-state routing (Dijkstra/SPF, used by OSPF) — fundamental classification of interior gateway routing protocols.

questions.question:
An IP router implementing Classless Inter-domain Routing (CIDR) receives a packet with address 131.23.151.76. The router's routing table has the following entries:

Prefix          | Outer Interface Identifier
----------------|---------------------------
131.16.0.0 /12  | 3
131.28.0.0 /14  | 5
131.19.0.0 /16  | 2
131.22.0.0 /15  | 1

The identifier of the output interface on which this packet will be forwarded is ______.
questions.to_find: Output interface identifier for longest prefix match of destination IP 131.23.151.76

understand.plain: CIDR routers use longest-prefix matching: for a given destination IP, the router checks every prefix in its table and selects the entry whose prefix matches the destination and has the most bits (longest prefix). We need to convert the destination and each prefix to binary and check how many bits match.

keywords.explain: An IP addressing scheme that replaces classful (A/B/C) addressing. Prefixes are expressed as IP/length (e.g. 131.22.0.0/15), meaning the first 15 bits identify the network.
keywords.example: /15 means the first 15 bits of the IP address are the network portion.

keywords.explain: When multiple routing table entries match a destination IP, the router forwards the packet using the entry with the most matching bits (longest prefix).
keywords.example: If /12 and /15 both match, the /15 entry is chosen because it is more specific.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe 10000011.00010111.10010111.01001100</text><rect x='10' y='62' width='500' height='26' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='15' y='80' class='tag ink'>Prefix</text><text x='220' y='80' class='tag ink'>First N bits of dest</text><text x='400' y='80' class='tag ink'>Match?</text><rect x='10' y='90' width='500' height='24' rx='3' class='bg'/><text x='15' y='107' class='sub ink'>131.16.0.0/12  10000011.0001</text><text x='340' y='107' class='sub ink'>10000011.0001</text><text x='420' y='107' class='sub ok'>YES (if/12)</text><rect x='10' y='116' width='500' height='24' rx='3' class='bg'/><text x='15' y='133' class='sub ink'>131.28.0.0/14  10000011.000111</text><text x='340' y='133' class='sub ink'>10000011.000101</text><text x='420' y='133' class='sub no'>NO</text><rect x='10' y='142' width='500' height='24' rx='3' class='bg'/><text x='15' y='159' class='sub ink'>131.19.0.0/16  10000011.00010011</text><text x='340' y='159' class='sub ink'>10000011.00010111</text><text x='420' y='159' class='sub no'>NO</text><rect x='10' y='168' width='500' height='24' rx='3' class='hi'/><text x='15' y='185' class='sub ink'>131.22.0.0/15  10000011.0001011</text><text x='340' y='185' class='sub ink'>10000011.0001011</text><text x='420' y='185' class='sub ok'>YES (/15)</text><text x='20' y='210' class='tag ink'>Longest match: /15 -> Interface 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Binary comparison of destination IP 131.23.151.76 against each routing table prefix. /12 and /15 both match but /15 is longer, so interface 1 is selected.

given.aim: Find the output interface using longest-prefix matching for destination IP 131.23.151.76

terms.connects: We compare the first N bits of this address against each prefix entry
This entry matches but is less specific than /15
This is the longest matching prefix, so interface 1 is selected

terms.example: Dest first 14 bits: 10000011.000101 — does NOT match 10000011.000111
terms.connects: This entry does not match the destination

given.plan: Convert destination IP and each prefix to binary. For each prefix of length N, compare the first N bits of the destination with the prefix. Among all matching entries, select the one with the longest prefix (most bits). That entry's interface is the answer.

steps.title: Convert destination IP to binary
steps.note: Only the first 16 bits matter since all prefixes are /12, /14, /15, /16.

steps.title: Check prefix 131.16.0.0 /12
steps.apply:
131.16 = 10000011.00010000
First 12 bits of prefix: 10000011.0001
First 12 bits of dest:   10000011.0001
Match: YES -> Interface 3 is a candidate.
steps.note: /12 means only the first 12 bits are compared.

steps.title: Check prefix 131.28.0.0 /14
steps.apply:
131.28 = 10000011.00011100
First 14 bits of prefix: 10000011.000111
First 14 bits of dest:   10000011.000101
Bit 14 of prefix = 1, bit 14 of dest = 0 -> NO match. Interface 5 not a candidate.

steps.title: Check prefix 131.19.0.0 /16
steps.apply:
131.19 = 10000011.00010011
First 16 bits of prefix: 10000011.00010011
First 16 bits of dest:   10000011.00010111
Bit 16: prefix=1 (0011->bit16=1), dest=1 (0111->bit16=1). Bit 15: prefix=1, dest=1. Bit 14: prefix=0, dest=1 -> NO match.
Actually: 131.19 second octet 00010011, dest second octet 00010111. These differ at bit 5 of the octet (0011 vs 0111). So bits 13-16: prefix=0011, dest=0111 -> mismatch at bit 14. Interface 2 not a candidate.
steps.note: 19 in binary is 00010011; 23 in binary is 00010111 — they differ at bit position 14 (counting from 1).

steps.title: Check prefix 131.22.0.0 /15
steps.apply:
131.22 = 10000011.00010110
First 15 bits of prefix: 10000011.0001011
First 15 bits of dest:   10000011.0001011 (from 00010111, first 7 bits = 0001011)
Match: YES -> Interface 1 is a candidate.
steps.note: 22 = 00010110, 23 = 00010111. First 7 bits of second octet: 22 = 0001011(0), 23 = 0001011(1) — first 7 bits are identical (0001011), so /15 matches.

steps.title: Apply longest prefix match
steps.apply:
Candidates: /12 -> Interface 3, /15 -> Interface 1.
Longest match is /15 (15 > 12).
Selected: Interface 1.
steps.note: CIDR always uses longest prefix match to select the most specific route.

solution.result: The output interface identifier is 1 (matched by prefix 131.22.0.0/15).

questions.formula_note: Longest Prefix Match (LPM): in CIDR routing, the routing table entry whose prefix has the most matching bits with the destination IP address is selected for forwarding.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements about the routing protocols, Routing Information Protocol (RIP) and Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) in an IPv4 network.

I.   RIP uses distance vector routing
II.  RIP packets are sent using UDP
III. OSPF packets are sent using TCP
IV.  OSPF operation is based on link-state routing

Which of the above statements are CORRECT?

A. I and IV only
B. I, II and IV only
C. I, II and III only
D. II, III and IV only
questions.answer: B. I, II and IV only
questions.to_find: Which statements about RIP and OSPF protocols are correct

understand.plain: This question tests detailed knowledge of two common interior gateway protocols: RIP and OSPF. We need to verify four specific claims — the routing algorithm used by RIP, the transport protocol RIP uses, the transport protocol OSPF uses, and the routing algorithm used by OSPF. Statement III is the trick: OSPF does NOT use TCP or UDP; it runs directly over IP.

keywords.explain: RIP messages (routing updates) are sent as UDP datagrams on port 520. UDP is used because RIP has its own timer-based reliability mechanism and does not need TCP's connection overhead.
keywords.example: RIPv1 and RIPv2 both use UDP port 520 for sending routing updates.

keywords.explain: OSPF packets are sent directly over IP using IP protocol number 89. OSPF does NOT use TCP or UDP. It has its own mechanisms for reliability (explicit acknowledgements for LSAs).
keywords.example: An OSPF Hello packet has IP protocol field = 89, with no TCP/UDP header.

understand.visual_alt: Protocol stack comparison: RIP runs over UDP (port 520) over IP; OSPF runs directly over IP (protocol 89) without TCP/UDP layer.

given.aim: Evaluate four statements about RIP and OSPF and identify which are correct

terms.term: Statement I: RIP uses distance vector routing
terms.meaning: RIP implements the Bellman-Ford distance-vector algorithm, sharing routing tables with neighbours
terms.example: RIP routers broadcast their full routing table to neighbours every 30 seconds
terms.connects: TRUE — this is a fundamental property of RIP

terms.term: Statement II: RIP packets sent using UDP
terms.meaning: RIP encapsulates its update messages in UDP datagrams, port 520
terms.example: RIPv1 broadcast, RIPv2 multicast 224.0.0.9, both over UDP/520
terms.connects: TRUE — RIP uses UDP for transport

terms.term: Statement III: OSPF packets sent using TCP
terms.meaning: OSPF does NOT use TCP; it runs directly over IP with protocol number 89
terms.example: OSPF Hello, DBD, LSR, LSU, LSAck packets all use raw IP (protocol 89)
terms.connects: FALSE — statement III is incorrect; OSPF uses neither TCP nor UDP

terms.term: Statement IV: OSPF based on link-state routing
terms.meaning: OSPF uses link-state algorithm: routers flood LSAs to build a complete topology map and run Dijkstra's SPF
terms.example: Each OSPF router computes its own shortest path tree using Dijkstra's algorithm
terms.connects: TRUE — OSPF is the canonical link-state IGP

given.plan: Evaluate each of the four statements against known facts about RIP and OSPF. I (TRUE), II (TRUE), III (FALSE — OSPF uses raw IP not TCP), IV (TRUE). Correct statements are I, II, IV -> option B.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I — RIP uses distance vector routing
steps.apply: RIP is a classic distance-vector protocol. Each router maintains a routing table and periodically shares it with directly connected neighbours. The algorithm is based on Bellman-Ford. Statement I is TRUE.
steps.note: RIP's metric is hop count; maximum is 15 hops (16 = infinity/unreachable).

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II — RIP packets sent using UDP
steps.apply: RIP (both v1 and v2) sends routing update messages encapsulated in UDP datagrams on port 520. This is well-defined in RFC 1058 (RIPv1) and RFC 2453 (RIPv2). Statement II is TRUE.
steps.note: RIP uses UDP (not TCP) since it does not require connection setup and handles its own timers.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement III — OSPF packets sent using TCP
steps.apply: OSPF does NOT use TCP or UDP. OSPF packets (Hello, DBD, LSR, LSU, LSAck) are sent directly over IP using IP protocol number 89. OSPF has its own built-in reliability mechanisms for LSA flooding. Statement III is FALSE.
steps.note: Common mistake: confusing OSPF with a protocol that runs over TCP. BGP uses TCP port 179, but OSPF uses raw IP.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement IV — OSPF based on link-state routing
steps.apply: OSPF is a link-state routing protocol. Each router floods Link State Advertisements (LSAs) describing its directly connected links throughout the OSPF area. Every router builds an identical Link State Database and runs Dijkstra's Shortest Path First algorithm. Statement IV is TRUE.
steps.note: OSPF's hierarchical area design (backbone area 0 + other areas) helps scale link-state flooding.

steps.title: Identify correct statements
steps.apply: Correct statements: I, II, IV. Statement III is FALSE. This matches option B: 'I, II and IV only'.
steps.note: The key discriminator is statement III — OSPF uses raw IP (protocol 89), not TCP.

solution.result: B. I, II and IV only

questions.formula_note: RIP: distance-vector, UDP port 520; OSPF: link-state, raw IP protocol 89 (not TCP/UDP) — standard GATE fact about interior gateway routing protocols.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements about the functionality of an IP based router.

I.   A router does not modify the packets during forwarding.
II.  It is not necessary for a router to implement any routing protocol.
III. A router should reassemble fragments if the MTU of the outgoing link is larger than the size of the incoming IP packets.

Which of the above statements is/are TRUE?

A. I and II only
B. II only
C. II and III only
D. III only
questions.to_find: Which statements about IP router functionality are true

understand.plain: This question tests understanding of what an IP router actually does when it forwards packets. A router DOES modify packets (it decrements TTL and recomputes the IP checksum). A router can use static routes and does NOT have to run a routing protocol. Reassembly of IP fragments is done ONLY at the destination host, never at an intermediate router — even if the outgoing MTU is larger.

keywords.explain: A field in the IP header decremented by 1 at each router hop. When TTL reaches 0, the packet is discarded (prevents infinite loops). Since the header changes, the IP checksum must also be recomputed.
keywords.example: A packet with TTL=64 entering a router leaves with TTL=63 and a new checksum.

keywords.explain: If a packet is larger than the outgoing link's MTU, a router may fragment it into smaller pieces. However, reassembly of fragments is performed ONLY at the final destination host, not at intermediate routers.
keywords.example: A 4000-byte packet may be fragmented by a router into three pieces, but only the destination host reassembles them.

keywords.meaning: A router can forward packets using manually configured static routes without running any dynamic routing protocol (RIP, OSPF, BGP, etc.).
keywords.explain: Small or stub networks often use static routes; no routing protocol is required.
keywords.example: A home router may have a default static route 0.0.0.0/0 -> ISP gateway without running OSPF or RIP.

understand.visual_alt: Router operations: (1) Decrements TTL and recalculates checksum — packet IS modified. (2) May use static routes — routing protocol is optional. (3) Fragments packets if outgoing MTU is smaller, but NEVER reassembles — only destination reassembles.

given.aim: Determine which of the three statements about IP router functionality are true

terms.term: Statement I: Router does not modify packets
terms.meaning: Claim that the router passes packets unchanged
terms.example: In reality, TTL is decremented and IP checksum is updated at each hop
terms.connects: FALSE — routers always modify the TTL field (and header checksum)

terms.term: Statement II: Routing protocol not necessary
terms.meaning: Routers can function with manually configured static routes without any dynamic routing protocol
terms.example: ip route 192.168.1.0/24 10.0.0.1 — a static route, no protocol needed
terms.connects: TRUE — static routing is a valid alternative to dynamic routing protocols

terms.term: Statement III: Router reassembles fragments if outgoing MTU > incoming size
terms.meaning: Claim that a router reassembles fragments when the outgoing path has a larger MTU
terms.example: RFC 791 and 815 clearly state: only the destination host reassembles IP fragments
terms.connects: FALSE — IP fragment reassembly is always done at the destination host, not at routers

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: I (FALSE — TTL decremented, checksum updated), II (TRUE — static routing does not require a routing protocol), III (FALSE — reassembly done only at destination, never at routers). Only statement II is true.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I — Router does not modify packets
steps.apply:
This is FALSE. An IP router ALWAYS modifies the IP header when forwarding a packet:
1. Decrements the TTL field by 1.
2. Recomputes the IP header checksum (because TTL changed).
These are mandatory operations per the IP protocol specification (RFC 791). The router may also perform fragmentation (modifying the packet further). Statement I is FALSE.
steps.note: Note that routers do not modify the payload (transport layer data), but they do modify the IP header fields TTL and checksum.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II — Routing protocol not necessary
steps.apply:
This is TRUE. A router forwards packets based on its routing table. The routing table can be populated by:
- Dynamic routing protocols (RIP, OSPF, BGP, EIGRP, etc.), OR
- Static routes manually configured by a network administrator.
For stub networks, small networks, or default routes, static routing is perfectly valid. No dynamic routing protocol is required. Statement II is TRUE.
steps.note: Many edge routers and home routers use only a static default route pointing to the ISP, with no dynamic routing protocol.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement III — Router reassembles if outgoing MTU > incoming size
steps.apply: This is FALSE. According to IP protocol design (RFC 791), IP fragment reassembly is performed ONLY at the final destination host. Intermediate routers NEVER reassemble fragments — not even if the outgoing link has a larger MTU. Routers may fragment packets (if outgoing MTU < packet size) but they do not reassemble. Statement III is FALSE.
steps.note: This is a classic trap: even though reassembly would seem 'helpful' on a larger-MTU link, the IP architecture explicitly reserves reassembly for destination hosts only.

steps.title: Identify true statements
steps.apply:
Statement I: FALSE (TTL decremented, checksum updated)
Statement II: TRUE (static routing does not require a routing protocol)
Statement III: FALSE (reassembly only at destination host)
Only Statement II is TRUE -> Option B.

questions.formula_note: IP router operations: TTL decremented + checksum updated at each hop (Statement I false); static routing eliminates need for routing protocols (Statement II true); IP reassembly occurs only at destination host, never at intermediate routers (Statement III false).
